title: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing
subtitle: The Only Way to Guarantee Your Fair Share of Stock Market Returns
author: John C. Bogle
authorBio: John C. Bogle (1929–2019) was an American investor, business magnate, and philanthropist who founded The Vanguard Group in 1974 and created the world's first index mutual fund in 1975. A Princeton University graduate, he pioneered low-cost passive investing for individual investors. Fortune named him one of the four 'Investment Giants of the 20th Century' in 1999, and Time listed him among the world's 100 most powerful and influential people in 2004. His investment philosophy — buy and hold the entire market at rock-bottom cost — has been endorsed by Warren Buffett, Paul Samuelson, and Charlie Munger. He authored ten books including Common Sense on Mutual Funds and The Clash of the Cultures.
keyConcepts: Business reality (dividend yield + earnings growth) vs market expectations
The relentless rules of humble arithmetic
Speculation vs investment (Clash of the Cultures)
Three sources of stock returns: dividend yield, earnings growth, P/E change
notableQuotes: The simplest and most efficient investment strategy is to buy and hold all of the nation's publicly held businesses at very low cost.
The more the managers and brokers take, the less investors make.
Don't look for the needle in the haystack. Just buy the haystack!
The stock market is a giant distraction to the business of investing.
The real formula for investment success is to own the entire market, while significantly minimizing the costs of financial intermediation.
Successful investing is not easy. It requires discipline and patience. But it is simple. For it's all about common sense.
Time is your friend; impulse is your enemy.
When the market is high, you buy less. When it is low, you buy more. But you always buy.
warrenBuffettQuote: If a statue is ever erected to honor the person who has done the most for American investors, the hands-down choice should be Jack Bogle. For decades, Jack has urged investors to invest in ultra-low-cost index funds. He is a hero to them and to me.
charlesMungerQuote: John Bogle is living a useful life, and this book is a useful contribution to his fellow citizens. It is dangerous for investors to believe a lot of nonsense, and the nonsense destroyers are particularly helpful when, like Bogle, they never tire in their animosity toward folly.
